content.text: A Microsoft Entra ID tenant is the dedicated, isolated instance of the Entra ID service that an organisation receives when it signs up for any Microsoft cloud service — Microsoft 365, Azure, Dynamics 365, or Intune. Every tenant is identified by a globally unique UUID (the Tenant ID) and at least one domain name (the initial domain is <organisation>.onmicrosoft.com).

attrs.alt: Microsoft Entra ID tenant structure showing subscriptions, identity objects, and tenant-wide policies

content.text: The tenant is the security boundary in Microsoft's cloud. All identity objects — users, groups, applications, devices — live inside exactly one tenant. Azure subscriptions are attached to a tenant (not the other way around), meaning the same subscription's resources are governed by the tenant's identity and policy rules.
One organisation, one tenant is the recommended default. Multiple tenants create identity silos and increase operational complexity.
Tenant ID: immutable UUID assigned at creation; appears in every token and API call
Primary domain: <name>.onmicrosoft.com — cannot be changed; additional custom domains (contoso.com) can be verified and added
Directory: stores all objects (users, groups, apps, devices) scoped to this tenant
Subscriptions: zero or more Azure subscriptions associated with this tenant for billing and resource management
Policies: Conditional Access, password, external collaboration, and token lifetime policies apply tenant-wide

# Azure CLI
az account show --query tenantId -o tsv

# List all tenants the signed-in identity has access to
az account list --all --query "[].{Name:name, TenantId:tenantId, State:state}" -o table

# PowerShell (Az module)
(Get-AzContext).Tenant.Id

# From the portal: Entra admin center → Overview → Tenant ID (copy button)

Every tenant has an initial domain ending in .onmicrosoft.com that persists forever. You can then verify and add custom domains (e.g. contoso.com) using a DNS TXT or MX record to prove ownership. Custom domains are used for user principal names (UPNs), email addresses, and application redirect URIs.

# Step 1: add the domain to the tenant
New-MgDomain -Id "contoso.com"

# Step 2: retrieve the DNS verification record
Get-MgDomainVerificationDnsRecord -DomainId "contoso.com" |
    Select-Object RecordType, Label, @{n="Value";e={$_.AdditionalProperties.text}}

# DNS admin creates: contoso.com  TXT  MS=ms12345678  (TTL 3600)

# Step 3: verify ownership once DNS has propagated
Confirm-MgDomain -DomainId "contoso.com"

# Step 4: set as default UPN suffix (optional)
Update-MgDomain -DomainId "contoso.com" -IsDefault

# Azure CLI equivalent
az ad domain list --query "[].{Domain:id, IsDefault:isDefault, Verified:isVerified}" -o table

# Add domain
az rest --method POST     --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/domains"     --body '{"id": "contoso.com"}'

Until a domain is verified, users cannot be assigned UPNs on it. If you delete an unverified domain you can re-add it; you cannot delete a verified domain while users or groups still reference it as their UPN or email domain.
Tenant vs Subscription: The Key Distinction
This distinction confuses every Azure beginner. The tenant and the subscription are separate constructs with different purposes:
Deletes all identity objects; cannot be undone
Deletes all resources; can be recreated
Cannot change tenant ID; can rename
Can be transferred to a different tenant

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.subscription import SubscriptionClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
sub_client = SubscriptionClient(credential)

for sub in sub_client.subscriptions.list():
    print(f"{sub.subscription_id}  {sub.display_name:<40}  {sub.state}")
# Output:
# 11111111-...  Production                                Enabled
# 22222222-...  Development                               Enabled
# 33333333-...  Sandbox                                   Enabled

# Move a subscription to a different Entra tenant
# WARNING: all role assignments are deleted; managed identities are lost
az account management-group subscription remove     --name "<mgmt-group-id>"     --subscription "<sub-id>"

# Then in the target tenant, associate the subscription
az account set --subscription "<sub-id>"
az account management-group subscription add     --name "<target-mgmt-group-id>"     --subscription "<sub-id>"

Some organisations intentionally operate multiple tenants — for regulatory isolation, mergers, or separate product lines. Others end up with multiple tenants accidentally after acquiring companies. Microsoft's guidance is to consolidate where possible, but there are legitimate multi-tenant scenarios:
Government or sector mandate to keep data in specific geography
Separate tenant in target region; use B2B for cross-tenant collaboration
Acquired company has its own tenant
Short-term: B2B guest access. Long-term: migrate objects to primary tenant
Preferred: separate subscriptions in one tenant. Multi-tenant only if regulations require
SaaS app that signs in users from any customer tenant
Single tenant for your identity; app registration marked multi-tenant
Students in one tenant, staff in another
Use Entra External ID or merge with dynamic groups controlling access

# Create a new Entra ID tenant (B2C or standard)
# Portal: Entra admin center → Manage tenants → Create

# PowerShell (requires Global Administrator in the parent account)
# Microsoft Graph does not expose a create-tenant API
# Use the portal or ARM template approach:

$body = @{
    countryCode   = "US"
    displayName   = "Contoso Dev Tenant"
    initialDomainPrefix = "contosodev"  # → contosodev.onmicrosoft.com
    tenantType    = "AAD"               # "AAD" (standard) or "AAD_B2C"
} | ConvertTo-Json

Invoke-MgGraphRequest -Method POST `
    -Uri "https://management.azure.com/tenants?api-version=2020-01-01" `
    -Body $body -ContentType "application/json"

# CLI: log in directly to a specific tenant
az login --tenant "contoso.onmicrosoft.com"

# Set a subscription in a specific tenant
az account set --subscription "<sub-id>"

# Python SDK: specify tenant explicitly
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential, AzureCliCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient

# DefaultAzureCredential uses the tenant of the active CLI context
# To target a specific tenant:
credential = AzureCliCredential(tenant_id="<target-tenant-id>")
rmc = ResourceManagementClient(credential, "<subscription-id>")
for rg in rmc.resource_groups.list():
    print(rg.name)
Several settings apply across the entire tenant and affect all users, apps, and guest accounts. These are configured in the Entra admin center or via Microsoft Graph.

import requests

# Read current external collaboration (B2B invite) settings
resp = requests.get(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authorizationPolicy",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"}
)
policy = resp.json()
print("Guest invite settings:", policy["allowInvitesFrom"])
# Values: none | adminsAndGuestInviters | adminsGuestInvitersAndAllMembers | everyone

# Update: restrict guest invitations to admins only
patch = {
    "allowInvitesFrom": "adminsAndGuestInviters",
    "allowedToSignUpEmailBasedSubscriptions": False,
    "allowEmailVerifiedUsersToJoinOrganization": False
}
requests.patch(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/authorizationPolicy",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=patch
)

# Set a custom token lifetime policy (requires Entra ID P1)
token_policy = {
    "definition": [
        '{"TokenLifetimePolicy":{"Version":1,"AccessTokenLifetime":"01:00:00","RefreshTokenMaxInactiveTime":"14.00:00:00"}}'
    ],
    "displayName": "Custom Token Lifetime",
    "isOrganizationDefault": True
}
resp = requests.post(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/tokenLifetimePolicies",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=token_policy
)
print(resp.status_code, resp.json().get("id"))

Deleting a tenant is irreversible and removes all users, groups, applications, and service principals permanently. Before deletion, Microsoft requires you to cancel all subscriptions and licenses, delete all applications, and ensure no resources remain.

# Pre-deletion checklist (run in the tenant to be deleted)

# 1. List and cancel all subscriptions
az account list --all --query "[?tenantId=='<tenant-id>'].{Name:name,Id:id,State:state}" -o table

# 2. Delete all enterprise applications / service principals
az ad sp list --all --query "[].{Name:displayName,Id:id}" -o table

# 3. Bulk-delete service principals (exclude Microsoft first-party apps)
az ad sp list --all --query "[?servicePrincipalType=='Application'].id" -o tsv |     xargs -I{} az ad sp delete --id {}

# 4. Delete all app registrations
az ad app list --all --query "[].{Name:displayName,AppId:appId}" -o table

# 5. Remove all custom domains (must have zero users on each domain first)
az rest --method GET --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/domains"     --query "value[?isDefault==\`false\`].id" -o tsv

# 6. Final deletion via portal: Entra admin center → Manage Tenants → Delete
Never delete a production tenant without a full identity export and a confirmed migration plan for all users, apps, and role assignments.
Default 30-day retention; export to Log Analytics for longer
Use $select to limit fields; avoid $expand on large tenants
Registrations with no sign-ins in 90+ days are candidates for removal
Azure Service Health → Active Events filtered by tenant
Subscribe to alert rules for identity-affecting incidents
Entra admin center → External Identities → Cross-tenant access
Control B2B inbound/outbound per partner tenant

When onboarding to a new tenant or auditing an existing one, the first step is a full configuration inventory — who are the Global Administrators, what domains are registered, what licensing is in place, and which external collaboration settings are active. The script below produces a structured JSON report.

import requests, json
from typing import Any

def graph_get(token: str, path: str, params: dict = None) -> Any:
    r = requests.get(
        f"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0{path}",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"},
        params=params or {}
    )
    r.raise_for_status()
    return r.json()

def tenant_inventory(token: str) -> dict:
    report = {}

    # Basic org info
    org = graph_get(token, "/organization")["value"][0]
    report["tenantId"]     = org["id"]
    report["displayName"]  = org["displayName"]
    report["createdAt"]    = org["createdDateTime"]
    report["country"]      = org["countryLetterCode"]

    # Verified domains
    report["domains"] = [
        {"domain": d["id"], "isDefault": d["isDefault"], "isVerified": d["isVerified"]}
        for d in graph_get(token, "/domains")["value"]
    ]

    # License SKUs
    skus = graph_get(token, "/subscribedSkus")["value"]
    report["licenses"] = [
        {"sku": s["skuPartNumber"], "assigned": s["consumedUnits"], "available": s["prepaidUnits"]["enabled"]}
        for s in skus
    ]

    # Global admins
    ga_role = graph_get(token, "/directoryRoles", {"$filter": "displayName eq 'Global Administrator'"})
    if ga_role["value"]:
        role_id = ga_role["value"][0]["id"]
        members = graph_get(token, f"/directoryRoles/{role_id}/members")["value"]
        report["globalAdmins"] = [{"upn": m.get("userPrincipalName"), "id": m["id"]} for m in members]
    else:
        report["globalAdmins"] = []

    # External collaboration policy
    auth_policy = graph_get(token, "/policies/authorizationPolicy")
    report["guestInviteSettings"] = auth_policy.get("allowInvitesFrom")
    report["allowEmailVerifiedUsers"] = auth_policy.get("allowEmailVerifiedUsersToJoinOrganization")

    # Conditional Access policy count
    ca_policies = graph_get(token, "/identity/conditionalAccess/policies")
    report["conditionalAccessPolicyCount"] = len(ca_policies.get("value", []))

    return report

inventory = tenant_inventory(access_token)
print(json.dumps(inventory, indent=2))
# {
#   "tenantId": "xxxxxxxx-...",
#   "displayName": "Contoso Ltd",
#   "domains": [{"domain": "contoso.com", "isDefault": true, "isVerified": true}, ...],
#   "globalAdmins": [{"upn": "admin@contoso.com", "id": "..."}],
#   "conditionalAccessPolicyCount": 12,
#   ...
# }

By default, guest users from partner tenants must be explicitly invited into your tenant. Cross-tenant access settings (introduced in 2022) let you configure fine-grained inbound/outbound trust relationships between specific partner tenants — including trusting their MFA claims so your users don't need to re-authenticate.
Configuring Cross-Tenant Access via Microsoft Graph

# List all cross-tenant access partner policies
az rest --method GET     --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/crossTenantAccessPolicy/partners"     --query "value[].{TenantId:tenantId, MFATrusted:inboundTrust.isMfaAccepted}"     -o table

# View default (applies to all tenants not listed as partners)
az rest --method GET     --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/crossTenantAccessPolicy/default"

Entra ID allows you to customise the sign-in page for your tenant — logo, background image, sign-in text, and username hint. This helps users confirm they are signing into your tenant and not a phishing page mimicking the Microsoft login experience.

import requests, base64

# Read an image file and base64-encode it for upload
with open("company_logo.png", "rb") as f:
    logo_b64 = base64.b64encode(f.read()).decode()

branding = {
    "signInPageText": "Welcome to Contoso. Sign in with your corporate account.",
    "usernameHintText": "username@contoso.com",
    "backgroundColor": "#1a1a2e",
    "squareLogoRelativeUrl": None,  # set via separate PATCH with binary content
}

# Update text-based branding
resp = requests.patch(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/organization/<org-id>/branding/localizations/0",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json=branding
)
print(resp.status_code)

# Upload square logo (binary PATCH to the media endpoint)
logo_resp = requests.patch(
    "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/organization/<org-id>/branding/localizations/0/squareLogo",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}", "Content-Type": "image/png"},
    data=open("company_logo.png", "rb").read()
)
print(logo_resp.status_code)
Entra ID Licensing Tiers and Tenant Features
The features available in a tenant depend on the Entra ID license assigned to users. Understanding this prevents you from designing an architecture that requires a license tier you haven't purchased.

# Check which Entra ID license SKUs are active in the tenant
az rest --method GET     --uri "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/subscribedSkus"     --query "value[].{SKU:skuPartNumber, Assigned:consumedUnits, Total:prepaidUnits.enabled}"     -o table

# Common SKU names:
# AAD_PREMIUM      = Entra ID P1
# AAD_PREMIUM_P2   = Entra ID P2
# ENTERPRISEPREMIUM = Microsoft 365 E3 (includes P1)
# SPE_E5           = Microsoft 365 E5 (includes P2)
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Using subscription owner as identity admin
No separation of duties; blast radius if account compromised
Use separate Global Admin accounts with PIM; never use subscription Owner for tenant admin
Forgetting to add custom domain before creating users
Users get UPNs on .onmicrosoft.com that are hard to rename
Verify custom domain first, then create users with correct UPN suffix
Creating a new tenant for dev/test
Identity silos, increased licensing cost, operational overhead
Use separate subscriptions in the same tenant with distinct resource groups
Leaving default external collaboration settings open
Any user can invite guests; phishing risk from uncontrolled guest accounts
Set allowInvitesFrom to 'adminsAndGuestInviters'; review guests quarterly
All role assignments (RBAC) deleted; managed identities lose permissions
Document all role assignments; re-create in target tenant before transfer

A Microsoft Entra ID tenant is the identity boundary for an organisation's Microsoft cloud presence. It holds all users, groups, apps, devices, and policies, while Azure subscriptions attach to the tenant for resource management and billing.
Tenant ID: immutable UUID; appears in every token, API URL, and sign-in log
Initial domain (name.onmicrosoft.com) persists forever; custom verified domains add human-readable UPN suffixes
Subscriptions are children of a tenant — transferring a subscription between tenants deletes all role assignments
Tenant-wide policies (Conditional Access, password, external collaboration) govern every object in the directory
Multi-tenant architectures are sometimes necessary but always increase operational complexity — consolidate where possible
Tenant deletion is permanent; cancel all subscriptions and migrate all apps before proceeding
